summary: Web-verify the factual claims in Robert's company-email draft and return citable footnotes (or flag unsupported)

results.finding: METR's published research confirms a real exponential trend in AI task time horizons, but the specific figures in the claim are substantially higher than what METR actually reports. METR's March 2025 paper ("Measuring AI Ability to Complete Long Tasks") found frontier models could complete tasks taking humans ~4 minutes in early 2023 and ~40 minutes in late 2024 (at the 50% success threshold). The most recent model tested as of late 2025 (Claude 3.7 Sonnet / Claude Opus 4.5 era) reaches approximately 1–5 hours. The doubling period is real: ~7 months over 2019–2025, accelerating to ~4 months (131 days under TH1.1) post-2023. METR's own forward projection is that if trends continue 2–4 more years from 2025, agents will handle "week-long tasks"; by decade's end, "month-long projects." The AI Digest's extrapolation puts "1 work day (8 hours)" around 2027 — not "weeks." The claim's figures of ~1.5 hours for 2023 and ~12 hours for 2024 appear to be ~20x and ~18x higher than METR's actual measured values for those periods.
results.source_title: Measuring AI Ability to Complete Long Tasks — METR
results.suggested_footnote: METR — "Measuring AI Ability to Complete Long Tasks", metr.org/blog/2025-03-19-measuring-ai-ability-to-complete-long-tasks/, March 19 2025; updated Time Horizon 1.1, metr.org/blog/2026-1-29-time-horizon-1-1/, January 29 2026.
results.caveat: The claim's specific benchmarks (~1.5 hours for 2023, ~12 hours for 2024, "weeks by 2027") do not match METR's published data. METR reports ~4 minutes for early 2023 and ~40 minutes for late 2024 at the 50% success threshold, with the most capable late-2025 models reaching roughly 1–5 hours. METR's own projection for 2–4 years out from 2025 is "week-long tasks," but the AI Digest extrapolation puts 2027 closer to "1 work day (8 hours)." The doubling-period figure (7 months historically, ~4 months recently) is accurate. The directional trend ("doubling rapidly," heading toward weeks of work) is supported; the specific hourly figures and the "weeks by 2027" endpoint are not.

results.finding: The closest verifiable source for the "8x" figure is Anthropic's own internal engineering data (reported ~June 2026), which shows that lines of code merged per active contributor at Anthropic reached approximately 8x the pre-2025 baseline — a span of roughly 18 months looking backward from Q2 2026. The data is specific to Anthropic's engineering team and measures code volume (lines merged), not general worker productivity. The "18 months" in the source is a retrospective baseline period, not a forward-looking window for how long the advantage will hold. No peer-reviewed or broadly-sampled study was found supporting an "8x" general productivity multiplier for humans working with AI. The largest broadly-cited multipliers in published research are: OpenAI's December 2025 enterprise report (6x more ChatGPT messages sent by power users vs. median users — a usage gap, not an output multiplier); Anthropic's own research showing Claude speeds up individual tasks by ~80% on average; and a 35% throughput lift in a large-scale customer-service RCT. No source found supports an "8x" advantage holding for the "next ~18 months" as a forward projection.
results.source_title: OfficeChai — "Anthropic Says That Their Employees Are Using AI To Write 8x More Code Compared To 18 Months Ago" (June 2026)
results.suggested_footnote: OfficeChai — "Anthropic Says That Their Employees Are Using AI To Write 8x More Code Compared To 18 Months Ago," https://officechai.com/ai/anthropic-says-that-their-employees-are-using-ai-to-write-8x-more-code-compared-to-18-months-ago/, June 2026. Note: OfficeChai does not cite the original Anthropic primary source document; treat as secondary reporting.
results.caveat: Three material gaps between the claim and the evidence. First, scope: the 8x figure applies to Anthropic's own engineering team measuring lines of code merged — not to humans working with AI in general. Second, metric: it measures code volume (a proxy), not holistic productivity. Third, time direction: the "18 months" is a lookback period (how far back the baseline sits), not a forward prediction that the advantage will hold for the next 18 months. The claim as written in the CEO email generalizes an internal, domain-specific, secondary-reported metric into a universal and forward-looking productivity law. No independent study corroborates "8x" for general workers; the best peer-reviewed evidence tops out at roughly 14–40% task-level gains for broad worker populations, and 6x only as a usage-intensity gap between AI power users and median users at the same firms.

results.finding: A January 29, 2026 Fortune article reports that Boris Cherny (Head of Anthropic's Claude Code) stated "100% for two+ months now, I don't even make small edits by hand," and that he shipped PRs "100% written by Claude." An Anthropic spokesperson put the company-wide figure at "between 70% and 90%." A pseudonymous OpenAI researcher named Roon separately stated "100%, I don't write code anymore." However, these are individual engineers and one spokesperson figure — Fortune does not report a statistic showing "most AI researchers" at frontier labs have stopped writing code. Google's figure at the time was cited in other sources as approximately 50–75% AI-generated, and the broader industry average was 30–42% AI-assisted.
results.source_title: Top engineers at Anthropic, OpenAI say AI now writes 100% of their code
results.suggested_footnote: Fortune — "Top engineers at Anthropic, OpenAI say AI now writes 100% of their code—with big implications for the future of software development jobs," https://fortune.com/2026/01/29/100-percent-of-code-at-anthropic-and-openai-is-now-ai-written-boris-cherny-roon/, January 29, 2026.
results.caveat: The claim that "most AI researchers have largely stopped writing their own code" is overstated compared to the evidence. The Fortune article documents two named individuals (Boris Cherny at Anthropic, Roon at OpenAI) who say they personally write 0% of code by hand, plus a company-wide Anthropic spokesperson figure of 70–90%. The article does not establish that "most" researchers across frontier labs have stopped writing code. Google's figure is separately reported as ~50–75% AI-generated, not 100%, and the broader industry sits at 30–42%. The strongest supportable version of this claim is: "Some senior engineers at Anthropic and OpenAI say AI now writes 100% of their code, with Anthropic's company-wide figure at 70–90%."

results.finding: As of January 29, 2026 — the most recent date with published scores from the Forecasting Research Institute's ForecastBench — the best AI systems (xAI Grok 4.20 Preview and Cassi ensemble_2_crowdadj) trailed human superforecasters by 0.017 Brier points (AI: ~0.102 vs. superforecasters: ~0.085–0.086). A linear extrapolation projects overall LLM-superforecaster parity for November 2026 (95% CI: January 2026 – November 2027), with the narrower "dataset questions" sub-category projected for ~June 2026. In March 2026, four DeepMind models briefly appeared above the superforecaster median on the preliminary leaderboard, but reversed as more questions resolved; the Forecasting Research Institute explicitly called this "noise in a small sample, not parity." As of April 2026 (the latest confirmed leaderboard data found), superforecasters remain ranked #1 overall. No authoritative source confirms that AI had durably matched the world's best human forecasters by May/June 2026.
results.source_title: Forecasting Research Institute — "LLMs Are Closing the Gap on Human Superforecasters"
results.suggested_footnote: Forecasting Research Institute — "LLMs Are Closing the Gap on Human Superforecasters," forecastingresearch.substack.com, January 29, 2026. ForecastBench leaderboard as of that date: superforecasters #1 (Brier ~0.085), best LLMs #2 (Brier ~0.102), gap 0.017 points. Overall parity extrapolated for November 2026.
results.caveat: The claim states AI "matched" the best human forecasters as of ~May/June 2026. The best available evidence shows AI was still trailing superforecasters as of late January 2026 and as of April 2026. A brief preliminary-leaderboard crossing in March 2026 reversed when more questions resolved and was characterized as noise by the benchmark maintainers. The "June 2026 parity" figure refers only to the narrower dataset-questions sub-category and is a projection, not a confirmed result. Full parity with the best human forecasters is projected for November 2026 at the earliest.

results.finding: In May 2026, OpenAI's internal reasoning model produced an original proof making a major advance on Paul Erdős's "unit distance problem," first posed in 1946 — approximately 80 years ago. The problem asks: given n points drawn on a flat surface, how many pairs can be positioned exactly one unit apart? For nearly 80 years, mathematicians believed the best constructions resembled square grids. OpenAI's model discovered an entirely new family of constructions that outperforms them, disproving Erdős's conjecture about the upper bound. Scientific American (published May 21, 2026) reported the result "would merit publication in a top math journal, as well as major media attention, even if it were performed by humans alone." TechCrunch (May 20, 2026) called it "the first time AI has autonomously solved a prominent open problem central to a field of mathematics." Thomas Bloom, who maintains the Erdős Problems website, endorsed the result. Days later, mathematician Will Sawin extended the finding, and a Google DeepMind model resolved nine related lesser Erdős problems.
results.source_title: AI Just Solved an 80-Year-Old 'Erdős Problem,' and Mathematicians Are Amazed
results.suggested_footnote: Scientific American — "AI Just Solved an 80-Year-Old 'Erdős Problem,' and Mathematicians Are Amazed," https://www.scientificamerican.com/article/ai-just-solved-an-80-year-old-erdos-problem-and-mathematicians-are-amazed/, May 21, 2026. Corroborated by TechCrunch, May 20, 2026: https://techcrunch.com/2026/05/20/openai-claims-it-solved-an-80-year-old-math-problem-for-real-this-time/
results.caveat: The claim is well-supported. Minor precision note: the problem was posed in 1946, making it ~80 years old as of 2026 (some outlets say "78-year-old"). The AI made a major advance — disproving Erdős's conjecture and finding better constructions — but this is technically a breakthrough on the problem rather than a complete closed-form solution to every aspect of it. "Solved" is the language used by OpenAI and major outlets, so the claim holds as written.

results.finding: In an October 28, 2025 livestream (reported by TechCrunch on the same date), Sam Altman said OpenAI is "tracking toward achieving an intern-level research assistant by September 2026 and a fully automated 'legitimate AI researcher' by 2028." Chief Scientist Jakub Pachocki described the intern-level system as one that can work autonomously for a bounded span of time, versus a full researcher that can handle open-ended, multi-week projects. No source describes the intern-level capability as equivalent to a "mid-PhD CS student" — that specific framing does not appear in any OpenAI statement found.
results.source_title: Sam Altman says OpenAI will have a 'legitimate AI researcher' by 2028
results.suggested_footnote: TechCrunch — "Sam Altman says OpenAI will have a 'legitimate AI researcher' by 2028," https://techcrunch.com/2025/10/28/sam-altman-says-openai-will-have-a-legitimate-ai-researcher-by-2028/, October 28, 2025.
results.caveat: Two discrepancies: (1) The timeline in the claim ("end of 2026") is close but not exact — the stated target is September 2026, which is mid-year, not end-of-year. (2) The "mid-PhD CS student" capability descriptor does not appear in any OpenAI statement found; sources only use the label "intern-level" without an academic equivalency. The CEO email should not assert the PhD framing as an OpenAI quote.

results.finding: Fable 5 (Anthropic's most capable publicly available model at launch) was released on approximately June 9, 2026 and suspended on June 12, 2026 — three days later — after the US Department of Commerce issued a directive received by Anthropic at 5:21 PM ET on June 12. The stated reason was NOT that the model was "too dangerous" in a general sense; it was a foreign-access / export-control restriction. The government cited national security authorities and claimed awareness of a method to bypass Fable 5's safeguards, but provided only verbal notice of "a potential narrow, non-universal jailbreak" with no specific written disclosure. Because Anthropic had no reliable real-time way to segment foreign nationals from US persons across its user base, it suspended access globally for all users. Anthropic publicly disputed the severity, arguing the alleged jailbreak was minor and reproducible with already-public models like GPT-5.5. A second model, Mythos 5, was also suspended under the same directive. Anthropic described the action as a "misunderstanding" and stated it was working to restore access. All other Claude models were unaffected.
results.source_title: Statement on the US government directive to suspend access to Fable 5 and Mythos 5
results.suggested_footnote: Anthropic — "Statement on the US government directive to suspend access to Fable 5 and Mythos 5," anthropic.com/news/fable-mythos-access, June 2026.
results.caveat: The claim says the government "forced it to shut down" because the model was dangerous — this is partially accurate but imprecise. The directive was formally framed as a foreign-access / export-control restriction (prohibiting foreign nationals from accessing the models), not a blanket safety recall. The underlying trigger was a reported jailbreak, not a broad "too dangerous" finding. Anthropic itself disputed whether the jailbreak was serious enough to warrant the action. Additionally, the claim says "a few days" — the accurate figure is three days (June 9 release, June 12 shutdown). The claim also omits that a second model, Mythos 5, was suspended simultaneously under the same order.

results.finding: The URL https://www.anthropic.com/institute/recursive-self-improvement resolves to a real Anthropic page titled "When AI builds itself." It does discuss AI autonomously conducting research and recursive self-improvement, but it does NOT make a specific "2028" prediction, and it does NOT claim AI will "match the smartest humans at everything doable on a computer" by that date. The page's most forward-looking statement is: "In 2027, AI systems could be capable of tasks that take a person weeks," and speculatively: "future versions of Claude could be continuously improved by Claude itself." It explicitly notes that "large performance gaps persist when it comes to Claude exercising judgement in choosing goals." The "2028" and "60% probability" framing comes from a separate essay by Anthropic co-founder Jack Clark in Import AI, where he pegs roughly 60% odds by end of 2028 that an AI could train a more powerful successor with no human involvement — a narrower claim than "conduct all its own research" or "match the smartest humans at everything." The "match the smartest humans at everything doable on a computer" language does not appear anywhere in the Anthropic page or in Clark's essay.
results.suggested_footnote: Anthropic — "When AI builds itself," anthropic.com/institute/recursive-self-improvement, June 2026. (Jack Clark's 2028 probability estimate appears in his Import AI newsletter, cited in coverage by The Decoder and Tom's Hardware.)
results.caveat: The claim bundles three things Anthropic has never stated together in one place: (1) a specific 2028 date, (2) AI conducting "all" its own research, and (3) AI matching "the smartest humans at everything doable on a computer." The URL resolves and is real, but the page does not support the 2028 date or the "match the smartest humans" framing. The 2028 figure (with a ~60% probability qualifier) comes only from Jack Clark's separate Import AI essay and applies narrowly to autonomous model training, not to general human-level performance across all computer tasks. Using this URL as a citation for the full claim as stated would be misleading.

results.finding: The Hackett Group (September 21, 2023) studied hundreds of components across 17 major SG&A end-to-end processes and concluded that generative AI "will have a profound potential impact on selling, general and administrative (SG&A) costs and staffing, enabling reductions of up to 40% over the next five to seven years." For a typical $10 billion company that translates to more than $180 million in annual SG&A savings. The covered functions are finance, procurement, HR, IT, marketing, and sales. Secondary aggregator sources (not independently verified to a fetched primary page) attribute a "20–30% operational cost reduction in targeted processes" to McKinsey's 2024 State of AI survey, but McKinsey's own pages timed out and could not be directly confirmed. No primary source found for "30–40% in the first year."
results.source_title: Generative AI to Cut SG&A Costs by 40% — The Hackett Group
results.suggested_footnote: The Hackett Group — "Hackett: Generative AI Will Drive Profound Reductions In SG&A Cost and Staffing — Up to 40% Over the Next Five to Seven Years," thehackettgroup.com, September 21, 2023. https://www.thehackettgroup.com/hackett-generative-ai-will-drive-profound-reductions-in-sga-cost-and-staffing/
results.caveat: Two mismatches between the claim and the evidence. First, the verified 40% figure applies to SG&A back-office functions (finance, HR, procurement, IT, marketing, sales) over a 5-to-7-year horizon — not to "routine operations" broadly in "the first year." Second, the lower bound of the claimed range (30%) appears in multiple secondary aggregator sites attributed to McKinsey and Gartner, but those primary report pages could not be loaded for direct verification. The claim's "first year" timeline is unsupported by any source found; the best sourced figure is a multi-year projection.

results.finding: The Stanford HAI 2026 AI Index Report documents that leading AI models now exceed the human expert baseline on PhD-level science benchmarks, achieved a gold medal at the 2025 International Mathematical Olympiad (Google Gemini Deep Think), and advanced on SWE-bench Verified (software engineering) from 60% to nearly 100% of human baseline in a single year. However, the same report introduces the concept of "jagged intelligence" — the identical models that outperform human chemists on ChemBench can read analog clocks correctly only ~50% of the time (vs. ~90% for humans on ClockBench). The report explicitly warns: "Capability is not uniform," and that AI surpassing humans on "most tasks" is not accurate — it is superhuman in narrow, well-defined benchmarks and below a child's level on others. A separate finding from the same report notes that over 50% of top models scored above the human baseline on Humanity's Last Exam, but human domain experts still average ~90% on that benchmark while the best AI sits around 35%.
results.source_title: Stanford AI Index 2026: State of AI in 12 Key Findings (summary of Stanford HAI 2026 AI Index Report)
results.suggested_footnote: Stanford Human-Centered AI Institute — "The 2026 AI Index Report: Technical Performance," Stanford HAI, April 2026, https://hai.stanford.edu/ai-index/2026-ai-index-report/technical-performance (summarized via AI Automation Global, https://aiautomationglobal.com/blog/stanford-ai-index-2026-state-of-ai-report, and IEEE Spectrum, https://spectrum.ieee.org/state-of-ai-index-2026).
results.caveat: The claim "on most tasks, AI makes fewer mistakes than humans" is significantly overstated. The best available evidence — the Stanford 2026 AI Index — shows AI exceeds human baselines on specific, well-defined benchmark tasks (PhD-level science Q&A, competition math, software engineering, image classification), but performs far below humans on others (analog clock reading: ~50% AI vs. ~90% human). The report's own framing is "jagged intelligence," not broad superiority. The claim needs to be scoped to "specific narrow benchmarks" rather than "most tasks." Additionally, Humanity's Last Exam results cut against the claim: human domain experts (~90%) still far outperform the best AI (~35% as of the 2025 benchmark run cited in the Index).
